what_it_does: Lists six small, grounded craft conventions from professional editing and review practice that could be adopted into the product's mark-up/review system.
observations: Item is filed as part of P7 and links to item-a72b23c2.
Proposes a global-scoped Change mark for house-style rules, treated as a sweep rather than a point edit.
Introduces 'Stet' as a one-tap author rejection of a proposed change that closes the thread as rejected-by-author.
Suggests a two-level severity/priority axis: must-fix versus nice-to-have.
Recommends structured answer widgets for member-to-author queries instead of free-text replies.
Argues the composer should show the verb and draft mark, visually anchored to the highlighted span, rather than re-quoting selected text.
Proposes arbitrary span selection that crosses block boundaries via an 'extend selection to here' gesture.
standouts: The 'Stet' concept as a professional, thread-closing rejection mechanism.
The shift from textual re-quotation to spatial/visual anchoring of marks.
The call for arbitrary, cross-boundary span selection beyond tree-based selection.
conventions: Follows the noticeboard item schema with YAML frontmatter and a markdown body.
Uses bold labels and bullet lists for rapid, scannable design notes.
References other items/threads by IDs (MSG2, A3, BD-D, BD-F, BD-G, BD-B) without defining them locally.
Treats professional editorial practice as a source of interaction design patterns.
concerns: Several cross-references (MSG2, A3, BD-D, BD-F, BD-G, BD-B) are undefined in this file, so their exact meaning is local-opaque.
The 'must versus nice-to-have' severity claim is presented as universal editorial practice without evidence.
The proposal to reserve an empty left/right slot for span extension assumes UI real estate that may not exist.
notes: A rapid-fire list of professional editorial conventions translated into specific interaction design proposals for the review/mark-up tool.
questions: What is the exact scope ladder referenced as A3?
What are the specific UI slots BD-B refers to?
How would global-scoped marks be reconciled with per-span mark rendering?
Would 'Stet' apply only to member-proposed changes, or to any mark?
What happens when a cross-boundary span is edited and the underlying blocks change?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to list smaller craft conventions worth stealing; the file does exactly that, so it matches.
apparent_themes: Reducing ambiguity in asynchronous author-member communication.
Scope and severity as first-class mark properties.
intention_signals: It is trying to enrich the review system's mark vocabulary and interaction patterns by importing established proofreading and legal-editor conventions, making triage and response more precise and less chat-like.
novelty: The synthesis of 'Stet' as a one-tap author rejection with thread-closing semantics, and the explicit call for cross-block arbitrary span selection in a gesture-based review interface.
contribution: A set of concrete, interaction-level proposals (global marks, stet, two-level severity, structured answer slots, visual mark anchoring, and cross-boundary spans) that a fused system could keep as candidate features for the review/mark-up tool.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard item listing six grounded editorial craft conventions—global house-style marks, stet, two-level severity, structured answer slots, visual mark anchoring, and cross-boundary span selection—proposed for adoption into a review/mark-up system.
intention_for_embedding: To propose small, professional review conventions as concrete interaction design improvements for the author-member markup workflow.
